discussion_comments.comment_body: I know this line originates from #4799, nevertheless I think we have to pre-process such things as `%%` so as not to include them in grammar / lexers at all if possible. Removing these lines beforehand will reduce overall complexity
totally agree.
%% lines are already removed by mermaid before parsing.

The parser should handle comments in general, it should be a standalone package.
I'm not sure if we should have that pre-process, but if we do, then it should be in the parser.

But how accurate it is? Some places it's possible to have `%%` in general. For example:

```
pie
"section
%% not comment
name": 1.0
```
And I want to highlight that if we modified the input, it would return an inaccurate AST in terms of each attribute value, position, and range.

I agree, if the new parser can handle the comments in a common manner, it should do it. 
We're already facing issues with incorrect line numbers in errors and stuff.
resolved in 3d3868a2bf08919673adc3fab42f70626e2f161e
The regex should match everything between `%%{` and `}%%`, if it's empty then just remove it.
ok but we have  every directive with init in it so that is why i used this regex pattern

Not necessarily, there are some other samples. It also can have empty spaces between after `%%{`, so do not rely on its content.

This one is escaped `\}`, but this one is not `{` (probably, should be).

discussion_comments.comment_body:
It is not clear why this complex regex is necessary. It feels like there should be an easier way.
When comparing with the deleted `.jison` file, the langium one is a lot more complex. Which makes it harder for people to create new diagrams. 

Something similar is duplicated inside the `.langium` file too. 
```
terminal SANKEY_LINK_VALUE returns number: /,("(0|[1-9][0-9]*)(\.[0-9]+)?"|[\t ]*(0|[1-9][0-9]*)(\.[0-9]+)?)/;
terminal SANKEY_LINK_TARGET: /,("((?:""|[^"])+)"|[^\n\r,]+)/;
```

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Matchers (most if the time) are just regexes to extract wanted data from tokens.

So here in these matcher regexes, it should match what is between doable quotes without including the wrapper doable quotes or everything until `,`.
It could be simplified and replace the first and last char, but we'll need to make sure which pattern has been matched.

---

As for `.langium`, I'm not sure if we could simplify it without allowing weird pattern.
So in the value regex, disallowed some patterns:

```
, "0" %% no space after "
,0.   %% no empty `.`
,00   %% first digit 0 can't have another number after it
```

We can just use `\d+\.?\d*` but I wouldn't recommend it.


As for the second terminal rule, I'm not if we could simplify the doable quote part.
I think `state` in `jison` are similar to `mode` in `chevrotain`, so when the use `"` it should enters a state where it should consume everything until it finds another `"`, but I don't know how to use the same rule to enter and exist mode.
But with modes the following code would be invalid (where is should be since `sankey` supposed to be `csv` like grammar):

```
sankey-beta
source",target,0
```

discussion_comments.comment_body:
> * That is not clear why commas should be part of these regexps, while they are very likely must be a part of grammar (not necessarily), but they are not necessary here

They are part of the grammar.

> Matchers (most if the time) are just regexes to extract wanted data from tokens.
>
> https://github.com/mermaid-js/mermaid/pull/4799#discussion_r1313973878

So for example, the lexer would tokenize the follow code:

```
wow, awesome, 10
```

into:

```
wow       ==> SANKEY_LINK_SOURCE
, awesome ==> SANKEY_LINK_TARGET
, 10      ==> SANKEY_LINK_VALUE
```

So I created these regexes that extract the data.
They could be replaced with some if-else and slicing.


>   * additional spaces around values


The rule already matches spaces before values, and there a hidden rule that ignores space at the end of values

https://github.com/mermaid-js/mermaid/blob/b6906a6474e01b5a6946749700150a4bada61657/packages/parser/src/language/sankey/sankey.langium#L20

> and at the beginning and at the end of strings

If I understand correctly, the rule would match them. Could you give an example?

we have to ensure that
```
 a , b , 10 
 "a" , "b" , 123
```
works

Yes, I see that this is part of lexer.
Initial grammar was like:
```
node comma node comma value
```
now grammar becomes
```
node node value
```
I am not against this, but it seems to me that regexp grabs something that is not related directly to the node, or value (comma). Comma is simply a delimiter, so the first approach seems more correct to me.

From my perspective it would be better to create a matcher for a comma

discussion_comments.comment_body:
> we have to ensure that
> 
> ```
>  a , b , 10 
>  "a" , "b" , 123
> ```
> 
> works

In CSV, the second link would be:

| first column | second column | third column |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| `"a"` | `"b"` | `123` |

Not just strings without doable quotations, and it's the current approach.

I'm not sure what you mean by ensure, we already have test cases that cover that.

> Yes, I see that this is part of lexer. Initial grammar was like:
> 
> ```
> node comma node comma value
> ```
> 
> now grammar becomes
> 
> ```
> node node value
> ```
> 
> I am not against this, but it seems to me that regexp grabs something that is not related directly to the node, or value (comma). Comma is simply a delimiter, so the first approach seems more correct to me.
> 
> From my perspective it would be better to create a matcher for a comma

Originally, we had a rule for comma, but I removed it.

In the jison parser, it uses states to shorten possible rules within the current context, e.g., if a `source` has been matched, then the possible rule after it is a comma; if it doesn't find a comma, it throws an error.
In langium parser (or chevrotain to specific), it has modes, it's similar states in jison, but I wouldn't recommend using it unless we actually needs to.

Anyway, if we created a rule for commas, how would we be able to distinguish between a `source` and a `target` rules? Both of them would have identical rules; they both match everything except `,` and newlines. We can't use context without modes in langium.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
In case you may wonder why I'm against using modes, it's because the default parser or lexer errors might be weird and not understandable.
And modes aren't part of grammar (`.langium` file); they're implemented in `TokenBuilder`, which would diverge and make it more complicated to adjust more services for straight-forward grammar.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
I think better example is a classic one:
```
expression ::= number '+' number | number '-' number;
```
left and the right operands are recognized by the same regular expression and there is not difference between them.
`+` and `-` are not the part of the numbers, unless there is additional unary operator.

Another example is:
```
assignment :== variable '=' variable | variable '=' expression;
```
There is no difference in terms of lexems. Both, left part of assignment and right part of assignment can be the same lexem. '=' is not the sub-element of the right part of the assignment. Parsers usually add additional attributes to lexems  like exact match, value etc.

So my point is that when you recognize `source` and `target` you are still recognizing `node`, they are identical, and their regular expressions are identical.
Otherwise we could as well use one regexp and capture groups  to recognize the whole string.
